request.question: What consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom that is located in Warrington, England , it has a park that  is now part of the resort, and there are  three locations opened by Gullivers?

title: Amusement park
American Waterfront (Tokyo DisneySea)
Leofoo Village Theme Park
List of former Hersheypark attractions
Gulliver's Land
Safari World
Scandia Amusements
Legoland Windsor Resort
Gulliver's World
Minnesota Zoo
sentences: An amusement park is a park that features various attractions, such as rides and games, as well as other events for entertainment purposes.
A theme park is a type of amusement park that bases its structures and attractions around a central theme, often featuring multiple areas with different themes.
Unlike temporary and mobile funfairs and carnivals, amusement parks are stationary and built for long-lasting operation.
They are more elaborate than city parks and playgrounds, usually providing attractions that cater to a variety of age groups.
While amusement parks often contain themed areas, theme parks place a heavier focus with more intricately-designed themes that revolve around a particular subject or group of subjects.
American Waterfront is a "port-of-call" (themed land) at Tokyo DisneySea in the Tokyo Disney Resort.

It represents the Northeastern seaboard of the United States in the early 20th century, and features two themed areas, an Old Cape Cod section, and a New York Harbor section with an elaborate backstory.
The Leofoo Village Theme Park () is a theme park and a safari located in Guanxi Township, Hsinchu County, Taiwan.

It features three roller coasters, including an inverted shuttle coaster, Screaming Condor, an Intamin twist-and-turn coaster dubbed Sahara Twist, as well as a Vekoma Roller Skater (335m) in the Wild West section of the park, Little Rattler, themed to an old mining railway.
There is also the Nairobi Express, a narrow gauge railway built by Severn Lamb.
The park features many other attractions of different styles and proper themed areas.
Hersheypark (operating as Hershey Park through 1970) is an amusement park located in Hershey, Derry Township, Pennsylvania.
The park was formally opened by Milton S. Hershey on May 30, 1906, and it became an entity of Hershey Estates when the Estates company was established in 1927.
From its opening in 1906 until 1970, the park was an open gate park.
In 1971, the park was gated and an entry fee charged.
This was the first preparations for the renovation project designed by R. Duell and Associates that would begin in 1972.
Since then, the park grew into the park it is today.
Casualties of these different changes are the rides and attractions that were removed or replaced.
Below is a list of the different rides and attractions Hersheypark has removed, which includes kiddie rides, thrill rides, roller coasters, themed areas, funhouses and other attractions of amusement.
Gulliver's Land is a children's theme park in Milton Keynes, England.
It opened in 1999 and is the third park to be opened by Gulliver's.
(The other two parks are Gulliver's World and Gulliver's Kingdom).
The park has been designed especially for families.
The Adventurers Village is now part of the resort, providing themed accommodation and short break packages.
Gulliver's Land resort includes Splash Zone, NERF Zone, The SFEAR and Dinosaur and Farm Park.
Safari World is a tourist attraction in Bangkok, Thailand that consists of two parks named Marine Park and Safari Park, operated by Safari World Public Limited.
The park was opened in 1988 with a total area of 480 acres for its open zoo and 180 acres for its bird park.

A major renovation to enhance effectiveness of land use began on 17 April 1989 and its total area developed for the leisure park now consists of an open zoo and a marine park on 500 rai (approximately 200 acres) of land.

Scandia Amusements is a chain of Scandinavian-themed family amusement centers in California, including three locations: Scandia Fun Center in Sacramento, Scandia Family Fun Center in Victorville, and Scandia Amusement Park in Ontario.
All three locations are located adjacent to a major freeway, and include attractions such as an arcade, "Baltic Sea Bumper Boats", batting cages, miniature golf, and "Stockholm Raceway".

Scandia Amusement Park in Ontario is the only location to include a full amusement park, including two roller coasters, called the "Scandia Screamer" and "Little Screamer", along with twelve other amusement rides.
Legoland Windsor Resort, also known as Legoland Windsor, is a child-orentated theme park and resort in Windsor, Berkshire in England, themed around the Lego toy system.
The park opened in 1996 on the former Windsor Safari Park site as the second Legoland after Legoland Billund in Denmark.
In common with the other Legolands across the world, the park's attractions consist of a mixture of Lego-themed rides, models, and building workshops.
The park was acquired by Merlin Entertainments in 2005, which now operates the park, with the Lego Group retaining part ownership (30%).
The facilities are mainly targeted at children between three and twelve.
Gulliver's World is a theme park located in Warrington, England.
Gulliver’s World theme park consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom.
The park features rides and attractions that have been designed especially for children between the ages of 2 and 13.
Splash Zone and NERF Zone are two indoor attractions located next to the main theme park.
The Minnesota Zoo (formerly the Minnesota Zoological Garden), is an AZA-accredited zoo in Apple Valley, Minnesota.
When it opened on May 22, 1978<ref name="mnzoo.org/timeline">http://www.mnzoo.org/guests/birthday/index.asp retrieved October 22, 2006</ref> it was fairly revolutionary in its exhibit design.
The zoo, built in a suburbanizing rural area, had more space to house exhibits and was one of the first zoos to organize its animals by their living environment as opposed to their species.
Exhibits are arrayed in six themed areas, including three themed walking trails ranging from one to two miles in length:

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: What consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom that is located in Warrington, England , it has a park that  is now part of the resort, and there are  three locations opened by Gullivers?
hop1_docs: Gulliver's World | Gulliver's World is a theme park located in Warrington, England.  Gulliver’s World theme park consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom.  The park features rides and attractions that have been designed especially for children between the ages of 2 and 13.  Splash Zone and NERF Zone are two indoor attractions located next to the main theme park.

Gulliver's Land | Gulliver's Land is a children's theme park in Milton Keynes, England.  It opened in 1999 and is the third park to be opened by Gulliver's.  (The other two parks are Gulliver's World and Gulliver's Kingdom).  The park has been designed especially for families.  The Adventurers Village is now part of the resort, providing themed accommodation and short break packages.  Gulliver's Land resort includes Splash Zone, NERF Zone, The SFEAR and Dinosaur and Farm Park.

Gulliver's Kingdom | Gulliver's Kingdom (also known as Gulliver's Matlock Bath) is a theme park aimed at children aged 3 to 13 in the Derbyshire town of Matlock Bath, England.  Founded in 1978 by Ray Phillips, it is still owned by the Phillips family and now has sister theme parks; Gulliver's World in Warrington and Gulliver's Land in Milton Keynes, which opened in 1989 and 1999 respectively.  The park was originally created by Ray Phillips as a model village for his young children to enjoy, and it is close to the site of the Victorian Switchback rollercoaster ride (after which the theme park's main rollercoaster was named).

Legoland Windsor Resort | Legoland Windsor Resort, also known as Legoland Windsor, is a child-orentated theme park and resort in Windsor, Berkshire in England, themed around the Lego toy system.  The park opened in 1996 on the former Windsor Safari Park site as the second Legoland after Legoland Billund in Denmark.  In common with the other Legolands across the world, the park's attractions consist of a mixture of Lego-themed rides, models, and building workshops.  The park was acquired by Merlin Entertainments in 2005, which now operates the park, with the Lego Group retaining part ownership (30%).  The facilities are mainly targeted at children between three and twelve.

Safari World | Safari World is a tourist attraction in Bangkok, Thailand that consists of two parks named Marine Park and Safari Park, operated by Safari World Public Limited.  The park was opened in 1988 with a total area of 480 acres for its open zoo and 180 acres for its bird park.  A major renovation to enhance effectiveness of land use began on 17 April 1989 and its total area developed for the leisure park now consists of an open zoo and a marine park on 500 rai (approximately 200 acres) of land.

Lilliput Nunataks | The Lilliput Nunataks ( ) are three nunataks, from 600 to high and trending southeast–northwest, located 3 nmi north of Gulliver Nunatak on the east side of Graham Land, Antarctica.  The nunataks are snow free on their southeast sides.  They were charted by the Falkland Islands Dependencies Survey and photographed from the air by the Ronne Antarctic Research Expedition in 1947.  The name, from Jonathan Swift's "Gulliver's Travels", means land of small people and was applied by the UK Antarctic Place-Names Committee in association with Gulliver Nunatak.

Disney's Fort Wilderness Resort & Campground | Disney's Fort Wilderness Resort & Campground is a themed camping resort located in the Magic Kingdom Resort Area at the Walt Disney World Resort.  It officially opened on November 19, 1971.  The resort is adjacent to Bay Lake and Disney's River Country, a now-defunct water park.  The resort is also located near Disney's Wilderness Lodge.
response.summary_1: Evidence: "Gulliver's World is a theme park located in Warrington, England" and "Gulliver’s World theme park consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom." Also: "Gulliver's Land ... is the third park to be opened by Gulliver's" and "The Adventurers Village is now part of the resort" (passage links this to Gulliver's Land), and "Gulliver's Kingdom ... has sister theme parks; Gulliver's World in Warrington and Gulliver's Land in Milton Keynes." Bridge/missing fact: these three named parks (Gulliver's World, Gulliver's Land, Gulliver's Kingdom) are the Gulliver's parks referenced, but it is unclear whether the question seeks the specific Warrington park (Gulliver's World) or the Gulliver's parks chain. Next retrieval should (1) confirm that "The Adventurers Village is now part of the resort" refers to Gulliver's Land, and (2) confirm which single entity (Gulliver's World vs. the Gulliver's chain) the question intends.
response.hop2_query: Gulliver's World Lilliput Land Gully Town Safari Kingdom Warrington Gulliver's Land Adventurers Village now part of the resort Gulliver's World Gulliver's Land Gulliver's Kingdom three parks
hop2_docs: Gulliver's World | Gulliver's World is a theme park located in Warrington, England.  Gulliver’s World theme park consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom.  The park features rides and attractions that have been designed especially for children between the ages of 2 and 13.  Splash Zone and NERF Zone are two indoor attractions located next to the main theme park.

Gulliver's Land | Gulliver's Land is a children's theme park in Milton Keynes, England.  It opened in 1999 and is the third park to be opened by Gulliver's.  (The other two parks are Gulliver's World and Gulliver's Kingdom).  The park has been designed especially for families.  The Adventurers Village is now part of the resort, providing themed accommodation and short break packages.  Gulliver's Land resort includes Splash Zone, NERF Zone, The SFEAR and Dinosaur and Farm Park.

Gulliver's Kingdom | Gulliver's Kingdom (also known as Gulliver's Matlock Bath) is a theme park aimed at children aged 3 to 13 in the Derbyshire town of Matlock Bath, England.  Founded in 1978 by Ray Phillips, it is still owned by the Phillips family and now has sister theme parks; Gulliver's World in Warrington and Gulliver's Land in Milton Keynes, which opened in 1989 and 1999 respectively.  The park was originally created by Ray Phillips as a model village for his young children to enjoy, and it is close to the site of the Victorian Switchback rollercoaster ride (after which the theme park's main rollercoaster was named).

Glumdalclitch | Glumdalclitch is the name Gulliver gives his "nurse" in Book II of Jonathan Swift's "Gulliver's Travels".  In Book I, Gulliver travels to the land of Lilliput.  Leaving there, he travels to the land of Brobdingnag.  In Lilliput, Gulliver was a giant, and in Brobdingnag, he is a dwarf, with the proportions reversed.

Lilliput Nunataks | The Lilliput Nunataks ( ) are three nunataks, from 600 to high and trending southeast–northwest, located 3 nmi north of Gulliver Nunatak on the east side of Graham Land, Antarctica.  The nunataks are snow free on their southeast sides.  They were charted by the Falkland Islands Dependencies Survey and photographed from the air by the Ronne Antarctic Research Expedition in 1947.  The name, from Jonathan Swift's "Gulliver's Travels", means land of small people and was applied by the UK Antarctic Place-Names Committee in association with Gulliver Nunatak.

Brobdingnag | Brobdingnag is a fictional land in Jonathan Swift's satirical novel "Gulliver's Travels" occupied by giants.  Lemuel Gulliver visits the land after the ship on which he is travelling is blown off course and he is separated from a party exploring the unknown land.  In the second preface to the book, Gulliver laments that this is a misspelling introduced by the publisher and the land is actually called Brobdingrag.

Balnibarbi | Balnibarbi is a fictional land in Jonathan Swift's satirical novel "Gulliver's Travels".  it was visited by Lemuel Gulliver after he was rescued by the people of the flying island of Laputa.
response.summary_2: Gulliver's World is the theme park in Warrington, England, consisting of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom. Passages corroborate that the Gulliver chain comprises three parks — Gulliver's World, Gulliver's Land (whose Adventurers Village is now part of its resort) and Gulliver's Kingdom.
